'''Main enshrined deity''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime・Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Ooyatsuhime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}}''' (Ooyatsuhime Mikoto) '''Deity enshrined'' * Left Wakimiya: {{ill|lt=Goju Takekami|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=Goju Takekami|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}} (Itakeru Mikoto) *: Oyatsuhime no Mikoto's older brother deity, {{ill|lt=Ita-Kiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} enshrined deity. * Right side shrine: {{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime・Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Miyatomatohime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}} (Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto) *: The sister deity of Oyatsu Hime no Mikoto, and the enshrined deity {{ill|lt=Toumatsuhime Shrine|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}}.As the company name suggests, our company is said to be a shrine dedicated to '''{{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Ooyatsuhime|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}}''' (Oyatsuhime no Mikoto). Regarding Ooyatsuhime, in the 5th book of the ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' Jindaiki's Treasure Sword Appearance Stage (8th Stage of the First Jindai Period), it is described that she is the daughter god of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} (Susanoo). Also, the older brother god of Ooyatsuhime is {{ill|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru/Itakeru|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}, and the younger sister god is {{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime/Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Sakutsuhime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}} (Tsumatsu Both gods are currently enshrined at {{ill|lt=Itaikiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} (Itaikiso, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Tomatsuhime Shrine|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}} (3 Ronsha shrines), respectively. These three deities are said to be the gods who brought tree seeds to Kii Province, and in Kii they are collectively known as ``''Itae Sosanjin'''' {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. Furthermore, in the Jigihonki, ``{{ill|lt=Sendai Kojihonki|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751|1=Kujiki}}'', these three gods are said to be the gods enshrined by {{ill|lt=Kiokunizo|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}.According to the shrine's legend, there was once a vast forest in the area around our company, and the wood was used to build houses, so it was given the divine name of ``Oya.'' According to "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}", the name of the enshrined place, "Udamori", came from the company's shrine complex.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. === History === === Founding ===Founding date is unknown. Shrine biography"Shrine Furyu" ({{Harvtxt|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1987|p=46-47}}). According to .94|N|135|12|4.67|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|Location|name=Maezen Shrine/Kokake Shrine (first enshrined place?①)}}) It was originally enshrined in the ground, but in {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, the site was given to Ryojingu Shrine in 1616 and renamed to the ``Boar Forest'' in Shandong (Itaikeso, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|1 1|58.42|N|135|15|14.38|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Boar Forest (former enshrined place?②)}})Currently, Sansei Shrine, a shrine outside the precincts of {{ill|lt=Itaukeso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}}, is located in Ino no Mori. {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. After that, "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" written by {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} In the 2nd year ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the three shrines of Ita-Kiso, Oyatsuhime, and Tomatuhime were transferred from Ino-no-Mori to the old shrine north of the current shrine site (Kitano-no-miya, Wakayama City). Mount Hara, {{Wiki coordinates|34|16|17.9|N|135|15|2.4|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Old palace (former enshrined place or ③)}}) There are currently no remains of the old palace. , and later relocated to its current location.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}In the Kii Famous Places Zue, ``Mt. Mihara'' is written as ``Mt. Miharayama'', and there is a different legend that it is the place where the three Ita-kami deities descended from the heavens with the seeds of wood, and from there they were divided into different shrines. .Also, "Oyatsu Hime-sha Engi" cited in "Zoku Fudoki" "Kii Zoku Fudoki". In , I will post a separate story that states that the three Itaige deities chose their respective places to be enshrined from Tenuki Bridge (bridge of the heavenly world) and went to various places {{ill|lt=Amakudari|Q480175|WD=Q480175|lt=Amakudari|1=Amakudari}}, but Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto chose Hirata-go, descended there, ran a palace there, and was enshrined there. Although its relationship with our company is unknown, to the east of the shrine there is the Udamori Ruins, which is thought to be a {{ill|lt=ring-moat village|Q60995739|WD=Q60995739|lt=ring-moat village ruins|1=Moated settlements}} from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}. === Outline === ==== Ancient ==== The first reference to the history of the country as a shrine is in the aforementioned article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}})'Shoku Nihongi' article dated February 22, 702. In , it is said that the three shrines of Ita-Kiso, Oya-Tohime, and Toma-Tohime were separated."Shinsho Kaku Chofusho" {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}})"Shinsho Kakuchofusho" 10 (Shinji Shoke Fudo) Daido 1st year (806). In , seven {{ill|lt=Kobe (Private)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711|lt=Kobe|1=Kanbe (social class)}} (private houses attached to the shrine) were granted to ``Oyatsuhime Shrine'' in Kii Province {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. Related, {{ill|lt=Shohei (Japan)|Q1324874|WD=Q1324874|lt=Shohei|1=Jōhei}} ({{ill|lt=931|Q24789|WD=Q24789|1=931}}-{{ill|lt=938|Q24794|WD=Q24794|1=938}}) "{{ill|lt=Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}" is Kii Province {{ill|lt=Nakusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}} ``Oya-go'' is listed twice in , but one of these is ``Oya-Kobe'', which means our Kobe, and this Kobe is thought to have been distributed around Udamori. As {{ill|lt=Divine|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} In the first year of the year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), he was elevated to the rank of Jushii (Junior Fourth Rank) along with Itagesojin and Tomatsuhimejin.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} |Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Kiokuni|Q864053|WD=Q864053|1=Kii Province}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa County|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}} is listed as "Oya Tsuhime Shrine" Meishin University It will be listed at {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}} as ``Monthly Niinamatsuri|Q11516161}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninamesai|1=UNKNOWN}}, it is written that it was deposited in {{ill|lt=notebook|Q11481885|WD=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}}{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. In ``{{ill|lt=Kinokuni Shinmecho|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008|1=Kii no Kuni Jinmyō-chō}}'', the Tenjin is listed as ``Oya Okami, the fourth rank of Jushii,'' but it is different from Itakeso Okami, who rose to Shoichii (Senior First Rank), and has an open shrine {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}.Company biography, in April of {{ill|lt=Kanji|Q1193874|WD=Q1193874|1=Kanji (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1088|Q19463|WD=Q19463|1=1088}}), on the occasion of {{ill|lt=Emperor Horikawa|Q464267|WD=Q464267|1=Emperor Horikawa}}'s visit to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hohei|1=UNKNOWN}}, and in the first year of {{ill|lt=Choji|Q1150870|WD=Q1150870|1=Chōji}} ({{ill|lt=1104|Q12490|WD=Q12490|1=1104}}), there were {{ill|lt=Kanda|Q10855080|WD=Q10855080|1=Shinden (divine field)}} of 18{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=machibu|1=UNKNOWN}} (approximately 22 hectares) and 5{{ill|lt=machi (Unit)#Unit of Length|Q1185752|WD=Q1185752|lt=Town|1=chō}} There is a similar description in 'Ki Province Famous Places Zue' that it prospered due to the donation of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine land|1=UNKNOWN}} on all four sides (approximately 30 hectares). However, there is no other record of Emperor Horikawa's visit to Kumano. . ==== After the Middle Ages ==== According to the shrine biography , it declined after suffering from the {{ill|lt=Oei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} uprising (first half of the 16th century) and the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} military fire ({{ill|lt=Kishū Conquest#Hideyoshi's Kishu Attack|Q10855721|WD=Q10855721|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Kishu Attack|1=Battle of Kishuu}}). According to "Kanbunki" cited in "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}", in the early modern period it had two towns of Kanda (approximately 2 hectares). Although this Kanda existed until after the war, it is said to have disappeared due to {{ill|lt=agrarian reform|Q208128|WD=Q208128|1=Land reform}} in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}21 ({{ill|lt=1946|Q18610|WD=Q18610|1=1946}}). group="Note">However, in Kanbunki, it is written in the past tense, ``existed until recent times (omitted).'' . In addition, the same article mentions that the area around our company, approximately 500 {{ill|lt=stone (unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}}, is called the ``Kami no Ki'' and is thought to be the remains of a vast shrine complex from ancient times, but the details are not clear. In "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}," the company at the time was the birthplace deity of the two villages of Udamori and Kitano, but it was originally considered to have been the birthplace deity of the entire Hirata Manor to which these two villages belonged, and it is assumed that due to the separation of the Konishi, Nishitai, and Kita villages due to a conflict within the manor, only the two villages became the birthplace.In April of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |Q134917284}}, and in 1880|Q7806}}, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. Also, in 1911|Q2076}}, the Soja Shrine (formerly Village Shrine) located in Miyanoshi in the northern part of Wakayama City was {{ill|lt=combined enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}} (described later). After the war, it belonged to {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === God level === * Records of {{ill|lt=Divine rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in the history of the Six Kingdoms ** {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} On the 27th of the first month of the first year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), from the 8th grade of the junior fifth rank to the 8th grade of the lower rank of lower ranks (Junior 4th rank and lower rank of 8th rank) (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - The notation is ``Oya Tsuhime no Kami''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** Upper Juichii? (``{{ill|lt=Kinokuni Shinto Mecho|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008|1=Kii no Kuni Jinmyō-chō}}'') - Written as ``Oya Okami''. It is said to be an error in writing ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper.'' === About Soja Shrine, a joint shrine === Oyatsuhime Shrine is the Sosha Shrine (formerly Village Shrine) located in Miyanoshi, Kita Aza, Wakayama City, which was enshrined in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}44 ({{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076|1=1911}}). This Soja Shrine is a shrine that was also called ``Soja Daimyojin'' in early modern times, but the deity it enshrines is not clear (in ``Ki Meisho Zue'' it is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined) {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. {{ill|lt=Enkyo|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1746|Q7589|WD=Q7589|1=1746}}) "Nanki Shrine Record" and its subsequent "Ki Meisho Zue" indicate that the shrine name and enshrinement location is "Soja" in Kii. |Q1629088}} is close to the estimated location (present-day Fuchu, Wakayama City), so this Soja Shrine is believed to be {{ill|lt=Soja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} in Kii Province.{{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. On the other hand, ``Kii Zoku Fudoki'' denies this and states that the shrine that was enshrined in Nezuku Valley in Taisho Ochiai Village (present-day Ochiai, Wakayama City) was transferred from the Sōron of the Shōchu period {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. === Precincts === [[File:Oyatsuhime-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Shrine building]] As for the shrine building, the central main hall has an Oji-zukuri copper plate roof, {{ill|lt=facing worship|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} is {{ill|lt=kara gable|Q977293|WD=Q977293|lt=kenkara gable|1=Karahafu}}, and {{jal {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1987|p=46-47}} where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chigi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Katsuoki|1=UNKNOWN}} are placed in the ridge of ink|Shinsha}}. To keep out the fog, all four sides of the shrine are covered with wooden walls. The side shrines on the left and right are also small-sized {{ill|lt=Nagazuzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper plate roofing and have chigi and bonito trees {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1987|p=46-47}}. However, the Chigi of the left wakimiya (Isotake no Mikoto) is a soto-kege, and the chigi of the right wakimiya (Miyatomatohime no Mikoto) is an inward-kege. === Setsusha === [[File:Oyatsuhime-jinja keidaisha.JPG|thumb|200px|right|小箠]] '''Precincts end shrine''' * Small shrine '''Outside shrine''' * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitano, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|16|12.6|N|135|14|41.1|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Outside shrine: Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine}}) === Ritual organization === === Shinto priest === The priesthood of Oyatsuhime Shrine was held for {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}}-{{ill|lt=1592|Q6769|WD=Q6769|1=1592}}) It is said that the Tsuchiya clan ({{ill|lt=real surname|Q11520116|WD=Q11520116|1=honsei}}: Fujiwara clan) has been hereditary since around 1987|1987|p=46-47}}. However, in the "Kiokuni Famous Places Zue," when it was compiled in the late Edo period, the Shinto priest was the Mori clan, who claimed to be a descendant of Oyahiko no Mikoto, and the current generation is the 83rd generation from Oyahiko no Mikoto. === Parishioners === Oyatsuhime Shrine is said to be the local deity of six districts, including Udamori, where it is enshrined, Hironishi, Kitano, Nango, Nishitai, and Kita. |Q11454763}} was formed, but currently it only remains in the Nagaho district. According to "Kii Zoku Fudoki", {{ill|lt=yabusame|Q1886157|WD=Q1886157|1=Yabusame}}, {{ill|lt=sarugaku|Q1204318|WD=Q1204318|1=Sarugaku}} and {{ill|lt=dengaku|Q3022455|WD=Q3022455|1=Dengaku}} were once held as festivals on June 1st and September 21st (both according to the lunar calendar). The same book also states that the priests of Itae-so Shrine serve during the festivals held on the last day of October and November 16th (both according to the lunar calendar), and that in October they serve food such as red bean rice, persimmon mochi, fish, and sake. It is said that a {{ill|lt=portable shrine|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} from Itaukeso Shrine was {{ill|lt=transferred|Q11561919|WD=Q11561919|1=transferred}} to our company in November. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747|1=Wakayama (city)}}Udamori 59 '''Access''' * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} (JR West Japan) {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|1=Hanwa Line}} {{ill|lt=Kii Station|Q857023|WD=Q857023|1=Kii Station}} (about 15 minutes walk) * Nearest stop: {{ill|lt=Wakayama Bus|Q11417290|WD=Q11417290|1=Wakayama Bus}} Rokujutani Line Kawanaga Danchi Stop (about 5 minutes walk) === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Original source''': Introducing the primary historical sources for the items mentioned. {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist}} === References === * "Kii Zoku Fudoki" edited by the Kishu domain (reprinted by the Wakayama Prefectural Shinto Priest Enforcement Office), Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Department, 1910 * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Oya Tsuhime Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Prefecture and shrine Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088313/205}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Part 2"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 205-206. * Supervised by Naoichi Miyaji and Yoshiyoshi Saeki, "Great Dictionary of Shinto, Reduced Edition", {{ill|lt=Rinkawa Shoten|Q11612454|WD=Q11612454|1=Rinsen Shoten}}, 1969 ISBN 4-653-01347-0 (first edition was {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1939) * Shitomo Takaichi, Kii Meisho Zue 1 (revised edition by Rekishi Toshosha), Rekishi Toshosha, 1970 * {{Cite book|Japanese {{Wikicite|reference="Oyatsuhime Shrine"|ref ={{Harvid|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Soja Shrine Ruins"|ref={{Harvid|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1985|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011|WD=Q4093011|1=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten}} 30 Wakayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}|isbn=404001300X|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Oyatsuhime Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Oyago"|ref={{Harvid|Oyago (corner)|1985}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Shikinai Shrine Research Group|author=Kinya Tsuchiya|chapter=Oyatsuhime Shrine|year=1987|title=Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report Volume 23 Nankaido|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakukan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}}Publishing related to Oya Tsuhime no Mikoto in Kii ** {{ill|lt=Susa Shrine (Arita City)|Q11664501|WD=Q11664501|lt=Susa Shrine|1=Susa Shrine (Q11664501)}} ({{ill|lt=Arita City|Q657843|WD=Q657843|1=Arida, Wakayama}} Senda, {{Wiki coordinates|34|3|53.55|N|135|8|29.32|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Susa Shrine (Father God)}}) - The father god of Oya Tsuhime no Mikoto. ** {{ill|lt=Ita-Kiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} (Ita-Kiso, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|12|6.17|N|135|14|59.77|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Ita-Kiso Shrine (older brother)}}) - The older brother of Oya Tsuhime no Mikoto. ** Tsuma Tsuhime Shrine - Sister deity of Oya Tsuhime no Mikoto. Next 3{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}. *** {{ill|lt=Tomatsuhime Shrine #Tomatsuhime Shrine (Yoshire, Wakayama City)|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|lt=Tomatsuhime Shrine|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}} (Yoshire, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|12|18.49|N|135|14|6.79|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|place name|name=Kichire no Miyako Matsuhime Shrine (Sister God Ronsha)}}) *** {{ill|lt=Tomatohime Shrine #Tomatohime Shrine (Hirao, Wakayama City)|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|lt=Tomatohime Shrine|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}} (Hirao, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|12|30.30|N|135|14|49.90|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|Place name|name=Hirao no Miyako Matsuhime Shrine (Sister God Ronsha)}})*** {{ill|lt=Takazumi Shrine|Q17226307|WD=Q17226307|1=Takatsumi Shrine}} (Wakayama City Negi, {{Wiki coordinates|34|14|5.04|N|135|15|41.42|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Takazumi Shrine (Sister God's Ronsha)}}) === External link === {{Commonscat|Oyatsuhime-jinja}} * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1014 Oyatsuhime Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Shrine Agency) * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/520401.html Oyatsuhime Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto/Shrine Historical Materials Collection”) {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyatsuhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyatsuhime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shrines receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai offerings]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社|名おおやつひめしんしや]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧県社]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

'''Abbreviated genealogy of the three Ita-kei gods'''
The names of the gods are written in the Nihon Shoki, and the names of the shrines are written in the Engishiki. {{familytree/start|style=font-size:100%}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{ill|lt=Susano-o|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susa no Takashi|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
({{ill|lt=Susa Shrine (Arita City)|Q11664501|WD=Q11664501|lt=Susa Shrine|1=Susa Shrine (Q11664501)}})}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|+|-|-|.| }}{{familytree|border=0|01|02|03| 01={{ill|lt=50 Fierce God|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=50 Fierce Life|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}

'''Main enshrined deity''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime・Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Ooyatsuhime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}}''' (Ooyatsuhime Mikoto) '''Deity enshrined'' * Left Wakimiya: {{ill|lt=Goju Takekami|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=Goju Takekami|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}} (Itakeru Mikoto) *: Oyatsuhime no Mikoto's older brother deity, {{ill|lt=Ita-Kiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} enshrined deity. * Right side shrine: {{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime・Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Miyatomatohime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}} (Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto) *: The sister deity of Oyatsu Hime no Mikoto, and the enshrined deity {{ill|lt=Toumatsuhime Shrine|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}}.As the company name suggests, our company is said to be a shrine dedicated to '''{{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Ooyatsuhime|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}}''' (Oyatsuhime no Mikoto). Regarding Ooyatsuhime, in the 5th book of the ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' Jindaiki's Treasure Sword Appearance Stage (8th Stage of the First Jindai Period), it is described that she is the daughter god of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} (Susanoo). Also, the older brother god of Ooyatsuhime is {{ill|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru/Itakeru|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}, and the younger sister god is {{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime/Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Sakutsuhime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}} (Tsumatsu Both gods are currently enshrined at {{ill|lt=Itaikiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} (Itaikiso, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Tomatsuhime Shrine|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}} (3 Ronsha shrines), respectively. These three deities are said to be the gods who brought tree seeds to Kii Province, and in Kii they are collectively known as ``''Itae Sosanjin'''' {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. Furthermore, in the Jigihonki, ``{{ill|lt=Sendai Kojihonki|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751|1=Kujiki}}'', these three gods are said to be the gods enshrined by {{ill|lt=Kiokunizo|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}.According to the shrine's legend, there was once a vast forest in the area around our company, and the wood was used to build houses, so it was given the divine name of ``Oya.'' According to "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}", the name of the enshrined place, "Udamori", came from the company's shrine complex.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. === History === === Founding ===Founding date is unknown. Shrine biography"Shrine Furyu" ({{Harvtxt|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1987|p=46-47}}). According to .94|N|135|12|4.67|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|Location|name=Maezen Shrine/Kokake Shrine (first enshrined place?①)}}) It was originally enshrined in the ground, but in {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, the site was given to Ryojingu Shrine in 1616 and renamed to the ``Boar Forest'' in Shandong (Itaikeso, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|1 1|58.42|N|135|15|14.38|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Boar Forest (former enshrined place?②)}})Currently, Sansei Shrine, a shrine outside the precincts of {{ill|lt=Itaukeso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}}, is located in Ino no Mori. {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. After that, "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" written by {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} In the 2nd year ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the three shrines of Ita-Kiso, Oyatsuhime, and Tomatuhime were transferred from Ino-no-Mori to the old shrine north of the current shrine site (Kitano-no-miya, Wakayama City). Mount Hara, {{Wiki coordinates|34|16|17.9|N|135|15|2.4|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Old palace (former enshrined place or ③)}}) There are currently no remains of the old palace. , and later relocated to its current location.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}In the Kii Famous Places Zue, ``Mt. Mihara'' is written as ``Mt. Miharayama'', and there is a different legend that it is the place where the three Ita-kami deities descended from the heavens with the seeds of wood, and from there they were divided into different shrines. .Also, "Oyatsu Hime-sha Engi" cited in "Zoku Fudoki" "Kii Zoku Fudoki". In , I will post a separate story that states that the three Itaige deities chose their respective places to be enshrined from Tenuki Bridge (bridge of the heavenly world) and went to various places {{ill|lt=Amakudari|Q480175|WD=Q480175|lt=Amakudari|1=Amakudari}}, but Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto chose Hirata-go, descended there, ran a palace there, and was enshrined there. Although its relationship with our company is unknown, to the east of the shrine there is the Udamori Ruins, which is thought to be a {{ill|lt=ring-moat village|Q60995739|WD=Q60995739|lt=ring-moat village ruins|1=Moated settlements}} from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}. === Outline === ==== Ancient ==== The first reference to the history of the country as a shrine is in the aforementioned article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}})'Shoku Nihongi' article dated February 22, 702. In , it is said that the three shrines of Ita-Kiso, Oya-Tohime, and Toma-Tohime were separated."Shinsho Kaku Chofusho" {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}})"Shinsho Kakuchofusho" 10 (Shinji Shoke Fudo) Daido 1st year (806). In , seven {{ill|lt=Kobe (Private)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711|lt=Kobe|1=Kanbe (social class)}} (private houses attached to the shrine) were granted to ``Oyatsuhime Shrine'' in Kii Province {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. Related, {{ill|lt=Shohei (Japan)|Q1324874|WD=Q1324874|lt=Shohei|1=Jōhei}} ({{ill|lt=931|Q24789|WD=Q24789|1=931}}-{{ill|lt=938|Q24794|WD=Q24794|1=938}}) "{{ill|lt=Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}" is Kii Province {{ill|lt=Nakusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}} ``Oya-go'' is listed twice in , but one of these is ``Oya-Kobe'', which means our Kobe, and this Kobe is thought to have been distributed around Udamori. As {{ill|lt=Divine|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} In the first year of the year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), he was elevated to the rank of Jushii (Junior Fourth Rank) along with Itagesojin and Tomatsuhimejin.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} |Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Kiokuni|Q864053|WD=Q864053|1=Kii Province}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa County|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}} is listed as "Oya Tsuhime Shrine" Meishin University It will be listed at {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}} as ``Monthly Niinamatsuri|Q11516161}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninamesai|1=UNKNOWN}}, it is written that it was deposited in {{ill|lt=notebook|Q11481885|WD=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}}{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. In ``{{ill|lt=Kinokuni Shinmecho|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008|1=Kii no Kuni Jinmyō-chō}}'', the Tenjin is listed as ``Oya Okami, the fourth rank of Jushii,'' but it is different from Itakeso Okami, who rose to Shoichii (Senior First Rank), and has an open shrine {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}.Company biography, in April of {{ill|lt=Kanji|Q1193874|WD=Q1193874|1=Kanji (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1088|Q19463|WD=Q19463|1=1088}}), on the occasion of {{ill|lt=Emperor Horikawa|Q464267|WD=Q464267|1=Emperor Horikawa}}'s visit to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hohei|1=UNKNOWN}}, and in the first year of {{ill|lt=Choji|Q1150870|WD=Q1150870|1=Chōji}} ({{ill|lt=1104|Q12490|WD=Q12490|1=1104}}), there were {{ill|lt=Kanda|Q10855080|WD=Q10855080|1=Shinden (divine field)}} of 18{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=machibu|1=UNKNOWN}} (approximately 22 hectares) and 5{{ill|lt=machi (Unit)#Unit of Length|Q1185752|WD=Q1185752|lt=Town|1=chō}} There is a similar description in 'Ki Province Famous Places Zue' that it prospered due to the donation of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine land|1=UNKNOWN}} on all four sides (approximately 30 hectares). However, there is no other record of Emperor Horikawa's visit to Kumano. . ==== After the Middle Ages ==== According to the shrine biography , it declined after suffering from the {{ill|lt=Oei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} uprising (first half of the 16th century) and the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} military fire ({{ill|lt=Kishū Conquest#Hideyoshi's Kishu Attack|Q10855721|WD=Q10855721|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Kishu Attack|1=Battle of Kishuu}}). According to "Kanbunki" cited in "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}", in the early modern period it had two towns of Kanda (approximately 2 hectares). Although this Kanda existed until after the war, it is said to have disappeared due to {{ill|lt=agrarian reform|Q208128|WD=Q208128|1=Land reform}} in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}21 ({{ill|lt=1946|Q18610|WD=Q18610|1=1946}}). group="Note">However, in Kanbunki, it is written in the past tense, ``existed until recent times (omitted).'' . In addition, the same article mentions that the area around our company, approximately 500 {{ill|lt=stone (unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}}, is called the ``Kami no Ki'' and is thought to be the remains of a vast shrine complex from ancient times, but the details are not clear. In "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}," the company at the time was the birthplace deity of the two villages of Udamori and Kitano, but it was originally considered to have been the birthplace deity of the entire Hirata Manor to which these two villages belonged, and it is assumed that due to the separation of the Konishi, Nishitai, and Kita villages due to a conflict within the manor, only the two villages became the birthplace.In April of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |Q134917284}}, and in 1880|Q7806}}, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. Also, in 1911|Q2076}}, the Soja Shrine (formerly Village Shrine) located in Miyanoshi in the northern part of Wakayama City was {{ill|lt=combined enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}} (described later). After the war, it belonged to {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === God level === * Records of {{ill|lt=Divine rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in the history of the Six Kingdoms ** {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} On the 27th of the first month of the first year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), from the 8th grade of the junior fifth rank to the 8th grade of the lower rank of lower ranks (Junior 4th rank and lower rank of 8th rank) (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - The notation is ``Oya Tsuhime no Kami''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** Upper Juichii? (``{{ill|lt=Kinokuni Shinto Mecho|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008|1=Kii no Kuni Jinmyō-chō}}'') - Written as ``Oya Okami''. It is said to be an error in writing ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper.'' === About Soja Shrine, a joint shrine === Oyatsuhime Shrine is the Sosha Shrine (formerly Village Shrine) located in Miyanoshi, Kita Aza, Wakayama City, which was enshrined in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}44 ({{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076|1=1911}}). This Soja Shrine is a shrine that was also called ``Soja Daimyojin'' in early modern times, but the deity it enshrines is not clear (in ``Ki Meisho Zue'' it is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined) {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. {{ill|lt=Enkyo|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1746|Q7589|WD=Q7589|1=1746}}) "Nanki Shrine Record" and its subsequent "Ki Meisho Zue" indicate that the shrine name and enshrinement location is "Soja" in Kii. |Q1629088}} is close to the estimated location (present-day Fuchu, Wakayama City), so this Soja Shrine is believed to be {{ill|lt=Soja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} in Kii Province.{{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. On the other hand, ``Kii Zoku Fudoki'' denies this and states that the shrine that was enshrined in Nezuku Valley in Taisho Ochiai Village (present-day Ochiai, Wakayama City) was transferred from the Sōron of the Shōchu period {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. === Precincts === [[File:Oyatsuhime-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Shrine building]] As for the shrine building, the central main hall has an Oji-zukuri copper plate roof, {{ill|lt=facing worship|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} is {{ill|lt=kara gable|Q977293|WD=Q977293|lt=kenkara gable|1=Karahafu}}, and {{jal {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1987|p=46-47}} where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chigi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Katsuoki|1=UNKNOWN}} are placed in the ridge of ink|Shinsha}}. To keep out the fog, all four sides of the shrine are covered with wooden walls. The side shrines on the left and right are also small-sized {{ill|lt=Nagazuzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper plate roofing and have chigi and bonito trees {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1987|p=46-47}}. However, the Chigi of the left wakimiya (Isotake no Mikoto) is a soto-kege, and the chigi of the right wakimiya (Miyatomatohime no Mikoto) is an inward-kege. === Setsusha === [[File:Oyatsuhime-jinja keidaisha.JPG|thumb|200px|right|小箠]] '''Precincts end shrine''' * Small shrine '''Outside shrine''' * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitano, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|16|12.6|N|135|14|41.1|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Outside shrine: Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine}}) === Ritual organization === === Shinto priest === The priesthood of Oyatsuhime Shrine was held for {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}}-{{ill|lt=1592|Q6769|WD=Q6769|1=1592}}) It is said that the Tsuchiya clan ({{ill|lt=real surname|Q11520116|WD=Q11520116|1=honsei}}: Fujiwara clan) has been hereditary since around 1987|1987|p=46-47}}. However, in the "Kiokuni Famous Places Zue," when it was compiled in the late Edo period, the Shinto priest was the Mori clan, who claimed to be a descendant of Oyahiko no Mikoto, and the current generation is the 83rd generation from Oyahiko no Mikoto. === Parishioners === Oyatsuhime Shrine is said to be the local deity of six districts, including Udamori, where it is enshrined, Hironishi, Kitano, Nango, Nishitai, and Kita. |Q11454763}} was formed, but currently it only remains in the Nagaho district. According to "Kii Zoku Fudoki", {{ill|lt=yabusame|Q1886157|WD=Q1886157|1=Yabusame}}, {{ill|lt=sarugaku|Q1204318|WD=Q1204318|1=Sarugaku}} and {{ill|lt=dengaku|Q3022455|WD=Q3022455|1=Dengaku}} were once held as festivals on June 1st and September 21st (both according to the lunar calendar). The same book also states that the priests of Itae-so Shrine serve during the festivals held on the last day of October and November 16th (both according to the lunar calendar), and that in October they serve food such as red bean rice, persimmon mochi, fish, and sake. It is said that a {{ill|lt=portable shrine|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} from Itaukeso Shrine was {{ill|lt=transferred|Q11561919|WD=Q11561919|1=transferred}} to our company in November. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747|1=Wakayama (city)}}Udamori 59 '''Access''' * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} (JR West Japan) {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|1=Hanwa Line}} {{ill|lt=Kii Station|Q857023|WD=Q857023|1=Kii Station}} (about 15 minutes walk) * Nearest stop: {{ill|lt=Wakayama Bus|Q11417290|WD=Q11417290|1=Wakayama Bus}} Rokujutani Line Kawanaga Danchi Stop (about 5 minutes walk) === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Original source''': Introducing the primary historical sources for the items mentioned. {{reflist|group="hara"}} '''Source''' {{reflist}} === References === * "Kii Zoku Fudoki" edited by the Kishu domain (reprinted by the Wakayama Prefectural Shinto Priest Enforcement Office), Imperial Local Administration Society Publishing Department, 1910 * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Oya Tsuhime Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Prefecture and shrine Meiji {{Wikicite|reference="Oyatsuhime Shrine"|ref ={{Harvid|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Soja Shrine Ruins"|ref={{Harvid|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983}}}} * {{Cite Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}|isbn=404001300X|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Oyatsuhime Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Oyago"|ref={{Harvid|Oyago (corner)|1985}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Shikinai Shrine Research Group|author=Kinya Tsuchiya|chapter=Oyatsuhime related to Oya Tsuhime no Mikoto in Kii ** {{ill|lt=Susa Shrine (Arita City)|Q11664501|WD=Q11664501|lt=Susa Shrine|1=Susa Shrine (Q11664501)}} ({{ill|lt=Arita City|Q657843|WD=Q657843|1=Arida, Wakayama}} Senda, {{Wiki coordinates|34|3|53.55|N|135|8|29.32|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Susa Shrine (Father God)}}) - The father god of Oya Tsuhime no Mikoto. ** {{ill|lt=Ita-Kiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} (Ita-Kiso, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|12|6.17|N|135|14|59.77|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Ita-Kiso Shrine (older brother)}}) - The older brother of Oya Tsuhime no Mikoto. ** Tsuma Tsuhime Shrine - Sister deity of Oya Tsuhime no Mikoto. Next 3{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}. *** {{ill|lt=Tomatsuhime Shrine #Tomatsuhime Shrine (Yoshire, Wakayama City)|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|lt=Tomatsuhime Shrine|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}} (Yoshire, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|12|18.49|N|135|14|6.79|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|place name|name=Kichire no Miyako Matsuhime Shrine (Sister God Ronsha)}}) *** {{ill|lt=Tomatohime Shrine #Tomatohime Shrine (Hirao, Wakayama City)|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|lt=Tomatohime Shrine|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}} (Hirao, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|12|30.30|N|135|14|49.90|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|Place name|name=Hirao no Miyako Matsuhime Shrine (Sister God Ronsha)}})*** {{ill|lt=Takazumi Shrine|Q17226307|WD=Q17226307|1=Takatsumi Shrine}} (Wakayama City Negi, {{Wiki coordinates|34|14|5.04|N|135|15|41.42|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Takazumi Shrine (Sister God's Ronsha)}}) === External link === {{Commonscat|Oyatsuhime-jinja}} * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1014 Oyatsuhime Shrine] (Wakayama Prefectural Shrine Agency) * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/520401.html Oyatsuhime Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto/Shrine Historical Materials Collection”) {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oyatsuhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oyatsuhime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shrines receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai offerings]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社|名おおやつひめしんしや]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧県社]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

'''Abbreviated genealogy of the three Ita-kei gods'''
The names of the gods are written in the Nihon Shoki, and the names of the shrines are written in the Engishiki. {{familytree/start|style=font-size:100%}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{ill|lt=Susano-o|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susa no Takashi|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
({{ill|lt=Susa Shrine (Arita City)|Q11664501|WD=Q11664501|lt=Susa Shrine|1=Susa Shrine (Q11664501)}})}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|+|-|-|.| }}{{familytree|border=0|01|02|03| 01={{ill|lt=50 Fierce God|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=50 Fierce Life|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}

'''Main enshrined deity''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime・Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Ooyatsuhime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}}''' (Ooyatsuhime Mikoto) '''Deity enshrined'' * Left Wakimiya: {{ill|lt=Goju Takekami|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=Goju Takekami|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}} (Itakeru Mikoto) *: Oyatsuhime no Mikoto's older brother deity, {{ill|lt=Ita-Kiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} enshrined deity. * Right side shrine: {{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime・Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Miyatomatohime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}} (Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto) *: The sister deity of Oyatsu Hime no Mikoto, and the enshrined deity {{ill|lt=Toumatsuhime Shrine|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}}.As the company name suggests, our company is said to be a shrine dedicated to '''{{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Ooyatsuhime|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}}''' (Oyatsuhime no Mikoto). Regarding Ooyatsuhime, in the 5th book of the ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' Jindaiki's Treasure Sword Appearance Stage (8th Stage of the First Jindai Period), it is described that she is the daughter god of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} (Susanoo). Also, the older brother god of Ooyatsuhime is {{ill|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru/Itakeru|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}, and the younger sister god is {{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime/Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Sakutsuhime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}} (Tsumatsu Both gods are currently enshrined at {{ill|lt=Itaikiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} (Itaikiso, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Tomatsuhime Shrine|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}} (3 Ronsha shrines), respectively. These three deities are said to be the gods who brought tree seeds to Kii Province, and in Kii they are collectively known as ``''Itae Sosanjin'''' {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. Furthermore, in the Jigihonki, ``{{ill|lt=Sendai Kojihonki|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751|1=Kujiki}}'', these three gods are said to be the gods enshrined by {{ill|lt=Kiokunizo|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}.According to the shrine's legend, there was once a vast forest in the area around our company, and the wood was used to build houses, so it was given the divine name of ``Oya.'' According to "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}", the name of the enshrined place, "Udamori", came from the company's shrine complex.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. === History === === Founding ===Founding date is unknown. Shrine biography"Shrine Furyu" ({{Harvtxt|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1987|p=46-47}}). According to .94|N|135|12|4.67|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|Location|name=Maezen Shrine/Kokake Shrine (first enshrined place?①)}}) It was originally enshrined in the ground, but in {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, the site was given to Ryojingu Shrine in 1616 and renamed to the ``Boar Forest'' in Shandong (Itaikeso, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|1 1|58.42|N|135|15|14.38|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Boar Forest (former enshrined place?②)}})Currently, Sansei Shrine, a shrine outside the precincts of {{ill|lt=Itaukeso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}}, is located in Ino no Mori. {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. After that, "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" written by {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} In the 2nd year ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the three shrines of Ita-Kiso, Oyatsuhime, and Tomatuhime were transferred from Ino-no-Mori to the old shrine north of the current shrine site (Kitano-no-miya, Wakayama City). Mount Hara, {{Wiki coordinates|34|16|17.9|N|135|15|2.4|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Old palace (former enshrined place or ③)}}) There are currently no remains of the old palace. , and later relocated to its current location.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}In the Kii Famous Places Zue, ``Mt. Mihara'' is written as ``Mt. Miharayama'', and there is a different legend that it is the place where the three Ita-kami deities descended from the heavens with the seeds of wood, and from there they were divided into different shrines. .Also, "Oyatsu Hime-sha Engi" cited in "Zoku Fudoki" "Kii Zoku Fudoki". In , I will post a separate story that states that the three Itaige deities chose their respective places to be enshrined from Tenuki Bridge (bridge of the heavenly world) and went to various places {{ill|lt=Amakudari|Q480175|WD=Q480175|lt=Amakudari|1=Amakudari}}, but Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto chose Hirata-go, descended there, ran a palace there, and was enshrined there. Although its relationship with our company is unknown, to the east of the shrine there is the Udamori Ruins, which is thought to be a {{ill|lt=ring-moat village|Q60995739|WD=Q60995739|lt=ring-moat village ruins|1=Moated settlements}} from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}. === Outline === ==== Ancient ==== The first reference to the history of the country as a shrine is in the aforementioned article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}})'Shoku Nihongi' article dated February 22, 702. In , it is said that the three shrines of Ita-Kiso, Oya-Tohime, and Toma-Tohime were separated."Shinsho Kaku Chofusho" {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}})"Shinsho Kakuchofusho" 10 (Shinji Shoke Fudo) Daido 1st year (806). In , seven {{ill|lt=Kobe (Private)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711|lt=Kobe|1=Kanbe (social class)}} (private houses attached to the shrine) were granted to ``Oyatsuhime Shrine'' in Kii Province {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. Related, {{ill|lt=Shohei (Japan)|Q1324874|WD=Q1324874|lt=Shohei|1=Jōhei}} ({{ill|lt=931|Q24789|WD=Q24789|1=931}}-{{ill|lt=938|Q24794|WD=Q24794|1=938}}) "{{ill|lt=Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}" is Kii Province {{ill|lt=Nakusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}} ``Oya-go'' is listed twice in , but one of these is ``Oya-Kobe'', which means our Kobe, and this Kobe is thought to have been distributed around Udamori. As {{ill|lt=Divine|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} In the first year of the year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), he was elevated to the rank of Jushii (Junior Fourth Rank) along with Itagesojin and Tomatsuhimejin.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} |Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Kiokuni|Q864053|WD=Q864053|1=Kii Province}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa County|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}} is listed as "Oya Tsuhime Shrine" Meishin University It will be listed at {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}} as ``Monthly Niinamatsuri|Q11516161}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninamesai|1=UNKNOWN}}, it is written that it was deposited in {{ill|lt=notebook|Q11481885|WD=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}}{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. In ``{{ill|lt=Kinokuni Shinmecho|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008|1=Kii no Kuni Jinmyō-chō}}'', the Tenjin is listed as ``Oya Okami, the fourth rank of Jushii,'' but it is different from Itakeso Okami, who rose to Shoichii (Senior First Rank), and has an open shrine {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}.Company biography, in April of {{ill|lt=Kanji|Q1193874|WD=Q1193874|1=Kanji (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1088|Q19463|WD=Q19463|1=1088}}), on the occasion of {{ill|lt=Emperor Horikawa|Q464267|WD=Q464267|1=Emperor Horikawa}}'s visit to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hohei|1=UNKNOWN}}, and in the first year of {{ill|lt=Choji|Q1150870|WD=Q1150870|1=Chōji}} ({{ill|lt=1104|Q12490|WD=Q12490|1=1104}}), there were {{ill|lt=Kanda|Q10855080|WD=Q10855080|1=Shinden (divine field)}} of 18{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=machibu|1=UNKNOWN}} (approximately 22 hectares) and 5{{ill|lt=machi (Unit)#Unit of Length|Q1185752|WD=Q1185752|lt=Town|1=chō}} There is a similar description in 'Ki Province Famous Places Zue' that it prospered due to the donation of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine land|1=UNKNOWN}} on all four sides (approximately 30 hectares). However, there is no other record of Emperor Horikawa's visit to Kumano. . ==== After the Middle Ages ==== According to the shrine biography , it declined after suffering from the {{ill|lt=Oei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} uprising (first half of the 16th century) and the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} military fire ({{ill|lt=Kishū Conquest#Hideyoshi's Kishu Attack|Q10855721|WD=Q10855721|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Kishu Attack|1=Battle of Kishuu}}). According to "Kanbunki" cited in "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}", in the early modern period it had two towns of Kanda (approximately 2 hectares). Although this Kanda existed until after the war, it is said to have disappeared due to {{ill|lt=agrarian reform|Q208128|WD=Q208128|1=Land reform}} in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}21 ({{ill|lt=1946|Q18610|WD=Q18610|1=1946}}). group="Note">However, in Kanbunki, it is written in the past tense, ``existed until recent times (omitted).'' . In addition, the same article mentions that the area around our company, approximately 500 {{ill|lt=stone (unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}}, is called the ``Kami no Ki'' and is thought to be the remains of a vast shrine complex from ancient times, but the details are not clear. In "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}," the company at the time was the birthplace deity of the two villages of Udamori and Kitano, but it was originally considered to have been the birthplace deity of the entire Hirata Manor to which these two villages belonged, and it is assumed that due to the separation of the Konishi, Nishitai, and Kita villages due to a conflict within the manor, only the two villages became the birthplace.In April of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |Q134917284}}, and in 1880|Q7806}}, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. Also, in 1911|Q2076}}, the Soja Shrine (formerly Village Shrine) located in Miyanoshi in the northern part of Wakayama City was {{ill|lt=combined enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}} (described later). After the war, it belonged to {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === God level === * Records of {{ill|lt=Divine rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in the history of the Six Kingdoms ** {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} On the 27th of the first month of the first year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), from the 8th grade of the junior fifth rank to the 8th grade of the lower rank of lower ranks (Junior 4th rank and lower rank of 8th rank) (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - The notation is ``Oya Tsuhime no Kami''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** Upper Juichii? (``{{ill|lt=Kinokuni Shinto Mecho|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008|1=Kii no Kuni Jinmyō-chō}}'') - Written as ``Oya Okami''. It is said to be an error in writing ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper.'' === About Soja Shrine, a joint shrine === Oyatsuhime Shrine is the Sosha Shrine (formerly Village Shrine) located in Miyanoshi, Kita Aza, Wakayama City, which was enshrined in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}44 ({{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076|1=1911}}). This Soja Shrine is a shrine that was also called ``Soja Daimyojin'' in early modern times, but the deity it enshrines is not clear (in ``Ki Meisho Zue'' it is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined) {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. {{ill|lt=Enkyo|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1746|Q7589|WD=Q7589|1=1746}}) "Nanki Shrine Record" and its subsequent "Ki Meisho Zue" indicate that the shrine name and enshrinement location is "Soja" in Kii. |Q1629088}} is close to the estimated location (present-day Fuchu, Wakayama City), so this Soja Shrine is believed to be {{ill|lt=Soja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} in Kii Province.{{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. On the other hand, ``Kii Zoku Fudoki'' denies this and states that the shrine that was enshrined in Nezuku Valley in Taisho Ochiai Village (present-day Ochiai, Wakayama City) was transferred from the Sōron of the Shōchu period {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. === Precincts === [[File:Oyatsuhime-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Shrine building]] As for the shrine building, the central main hall has an Oji-zukuri copper plate roof, {{ill|lt=facing worship|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} is {{ill|lt=kara gable|Q977293|WD=Q977293|lt=kenkara gable|1=Karahafu}}, and {{jal {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1987|p=46-47}} where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chigi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Katsuoki|1=UNKNOWN}} are placed in the ridge of ink|Shinsha}}. To keep out the fog, all four sides of the shrine are covered with wooden walls. The side shrines on the left and right are also small-sized {{ill|lt=Nagazuzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper plate roofing and have chigi and bonito trees {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1987|p=46-47}}. However, the Chigi of the left wakimiya (Isotake no Mikoto) is a soto-kege, and the chigi of the right wakimiya (Miyatomatohime no Mikoto) is an inward-kege. === Setsusha === [[File:Oyatsuhime-jinja keidaisha.JPG|thumb|200px|right|小箠]] '''Precincts end shrine''' * Small shrine '''Outside shrine''' * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitano, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|16|12.6|N|135|14|41.1|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Outside shrine: Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine}}) === Ritual organization === === Shinto priest === The priesthood of Oyatsuhime Shrine was held for {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}}-{{ill|lt=1592|Q6769|WD=Q6769|1=1592}}) It is said that the Tsuchiya clan ({{ill|lt=real surname|Q11520116|WD=Q11520116|1=honsei}}: Fujiwara clan) has been hereditary since around 1987|1987|p=46-47}}. However, in the "Kiokuni Famous Places Zue," when it was compiled in the late Edo period, the Shinto priest was the Mori clan, who claimed to be a descendant of Oyahiko no Mikoto, and the current generation is the 83rd generation from Oyahiko no Mikoto. === Parishioners === Oyatsuhime Shrine is said to be the local deity of six districts, including Udamori, where it is enshrined, Hironishi, Kitano, Nango, Nishitai, and Kita. |Q11454763}} was formed, but currently it only remains in the Nagaho district. According to "Kii Zoku Fudoki", {{ill|lt=yabusame|Q1886157|WD=Q1886157|1=Yabusame}}, {{ill|lt=sarugaku|Q1204318|WD=Q1204318|1=Sarugaku}} and {{ill|lt=dengaku|Q3022455|WD=Q3022455|1=Dengaku}} were once held as festivals on June 1st and September 21st (both according to the lunar calendar). The same book also states that the priests of Itae-so Shrine serve during the festivals held on the last day of October and November 16th (both according to the lunar calendar), and that in October they serve food such as red bean rice, persimmon mochi, fish, and sake. It is said that a {{ill|lt=portable shrine|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} from Itaukeso Shrine was {{ill|lt=transferred|Q11561919|WD=Q11561919|1=transferred}} to our company in November. === Local '''Abbreviated genealogy of the three Ita-kei gods'''
The names of the gods are written in the Nihon Shoki, and the names of the shrines are written in the Engishiki. {{familytree/start|style=font-size:100%}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{ill|lt=Susano-o|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susa no Takashi|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
({{ill|lt=Susa Shrine (Arita City)|Q11664501|WD=Q11664501|lt=Susa Shrine|1=Susa Shrine (Q11664501)}})}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|+|-|-|.| }}{{familytree|border=0|01|02|03| 01={{ill|lt=50 Fierce God|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=50 Fierce Life|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}

'''Main enshrined deity''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime・Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Ooyatsuhime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}}''' (Ooyatsuhime Mikoto) '''Deity enshrined'' * Left Wakimiya: {{ill|lt=Goju Takekami|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=Goju Takekami|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}} (Itakeru Mikoto) *: Oyatsuhime no Mikoto's older brother deity, {{ill|lt=Ita-Kiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} enshrined deity. * Right side shrine: {{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime・Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Miyatomatohime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}} (Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto) *: The sister deity of Oyatsu Hime no Mikoto, and the enshrined deity {{ill|lt=Toumatsuhime Shrine|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}}.As the company name suggests, our company is said to be a shrine dedicated to '''{{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Ooyatsuhime|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}}''' (Oyatsuhime no Mikoto). Regarding Ooyatsuhime, in the 5th book of the ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' Jindaiki's Treasure Sword Appearance Stage (8th Stage of the First Jindai Period), it is described that she is the daughter god of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} (Susanoo). Also, the older brother god of Ooyatsuhime is {{ill|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru/Itakeru|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}, and the younger sister god is {{ill|lt=Ooyatsuhime/Tsumatsuhime|Q10935047|WD=Q10935047|lt=Sakutsuhime no Mikoto|1=Oyatsuhime and Tsumatsuhime}} (Tsumatsu Both gods are currently enshrined at {{ill|lt=Itaikiso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}} (Itaikiso, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Tomatsuhime Shrine|Q17216259|WD=Q17216259|1=Tsumatsuhime Shrine}} (3 Ronsha shrines), respectively. These three deities are said to be the gods who brought tree seeds to Kii Province, and in Kii they are collectively known as ``''Itae Sosanjin'''' {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. Furthermore, in the Jigihonki, ``{{ill|lt=Sendai Kojihonki|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751|1=Kujiki}}'', these three gods are said to be the gods enshrined by {{ill|lt=Kiokunizo|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}.According to the shrine's legend, there was once a vast forest in the area around our company, and the wood was used to build houses, so it was given the divine name of ``Oya.'' According to "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}", the name of the enshrined place, "Udamori", came from the company's shrine complex.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. === History === === Founding ===Founding date is unknown. Shrine biography"Shrine Furyu" ({{Harvtxt|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1987|p=46-47}}). According to .94|N|135|12|4.67|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|Location|name=Maezen Shrine/Kokake Shrine (first enshrined place?①)}}) It was originally enshrined in the ground, but in {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, the site was given to Ryojingu Shrine in 1616 and renamed to the ``Boar Forest'' in Shandong (Itaikeso, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|1 1|58.42|N|135|15|14.38|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Boar Forest (former enshrined place?②)}})Currently, Sansei Shrine, a shrine outside the precincts of {{ill|lt=Itaukeso Shrine|Q11379271|WD=Q11379271|1=Itakiso shrine}}, is located in Ino no Mori. {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. After that, "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" written by {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} In the 2nd year ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}), the three shrines of Ita-Kiso, Oyatsuhime, and Tomatuhime were transferred from Ino-no-Mori to the old shrine north of the current shrine site (Kitano-no-miya, Wakayama City). Mount Hara, {{Wiki coordinates|34|16|17.9|N|135|15|2.4|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Old palace (former enshrined place or ③)}}) There are currently no remains of the old palace. , and later relocated to its current location.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}In the Kii Famous Places Zue, ``Mt. Mihara'' is written as ``Mt. Miharayama'', and there is a different legend that it is the place where the three Ita-kami deities descended from the heavens with the seeds of wood, and from there they were divided into different shrines. .Also, "Oyatsu Hime-sha Engi" cited in "Zoku Fudoki" "Kii Zoku Fudoki". In , I will post a separate story that states that the three Itaige deities chose their respective places to be enshrined from Tenuki Bridge (bridge of the heavenly world) and went to various places {{ill|lt=Amakudari|Q480175|WD=Q480175|lt=Amakudari|1=Amakudari}}, but Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto chose Hirata-go, descended there, ran a palace there, and was enshrined there. Although its relationship with our company is unknown, to the east of the shrine there is the Udamori Ruins, which is thought to be a {{ill|lt=ring-moat village|Q60995739|WD=Q60995739|lt=ring-moat village ruins|1=Moated settlements}} from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858|1=Yayoi period}}. === Outline === ==== Ancient ==== The first reference to the history of the country as a shrine is in the aforementioned article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" {{ill|lt=Taiho (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taiho|1=Taihō (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}})'Shoku Nihongi' article dated February 22, 702. In , it is said that the three shrines of Ita-Kiso, Oya-Tohime, and Toma-Tohime were separated."Shinsho Kaku Chofusho" {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}})"Shinsho Kakuchofusho" 10 (Shinji Shoke Fudo) Daido 1st year (806). In , seven {{ill|lt=Kobe (Private)|Q11589711|WD=Q11589711|lt=Kobe|1=Kanbe (social class)}} (private houses attached to the shrine) were granted to ``Oyatsuhime Shrine'' in Kii Province {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. Related, {{ill|lt=Shohei (Japan)|Q1324874|WD=Q1324874|lt=Shohei|1=Jōhei}} ({{ill|lt=931|Q24789|WD=Q24789|1=931}}-{{ill|lt=938|Q24794|WD=Q24794|1=938}}) "{{ill|lt=Japanese name Ruijusho|Q2702542|WD=Q2702542|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}" is Kii Province {{ill|lt=Nakusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}} ``Oya-go'' is listed twice in , but one of these is ``Oya-Kobe'', which means our Kobe, and this Kobe is thought to have been distributed around Udamori. As {{ill|lt=Divine|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} In the first year of the year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), he was elevated to the rank of Jushii (Junior Fourth Rank) along with Itagesojin and Tomatsuhimejin.{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} |Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|Shin Name Book}}, {{ill|lt=Kiokuni|Q864053|WD=Q864053|1=Kii Province}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa County|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}} is listed as "Oya Tsuhime Shrine" Meishin University It will be listed at {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}} as ``Monthly Niinamatsuri|Q11516161}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninamesai|1=UNKNOWN}}, it is written that it was deposited in {{ill|lt=notebook|Q11481885|WD=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}}{{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}. In ``{{ill|lt=Kinokuni Shinmecho|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008|1=Kii no Kuni Jinmyō-chō}}'', the Tenjin is listed as ``Oya Okami, the fourth rank of Jushii,'' but it is different from Itakeso Okami, who rose to Shoichii (Senior First Rank), and has an open shrine {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Taira)|1983|p=444-445}}.Company biography, in April of {{ill|lt=Kanji|Q1193874|WD=Q1193874|1=Kanji (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1088|Q19463|WD=Q19463|1=1088}}), on the occasion of {{ill|lt=Emperor Horikawa|Q464267|WD=Q464267|1=Emperor Horikawa}}'s visit to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hohei|1=UNKNOWN}}, and in the first year of {{ill|lt=Choji|Q1150870|WD=Q1150870|1=Chōji}} ({{ill|lt=1104|Q12490|WD=Q12490|1=1104}}), there were {{ill|lt=Kanda|Q10855080|WD=Q10855080|1=Shinden (divine field)}} of 18{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=machibu|1=UNKNOWN}} (approximately 22 hectares) and 5{{ill|lt=machi (Unit)#Unit of Length|Q1185752|WD=Q1185752|lt=Town|1=chō}} There is a similar description in 'Ki Province Famous Places Zue' that it prospered due to the donation of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine land|1=UNKNOWN}} on all four sides (approximately 30 hectares). However, there is no other record of Emperor Horikawa's visit to Kumano. . ==== After the Middle Ages ==== According to the shrine biography , it declined after suffering from the {{ill|lt=Oei|Q1193564|WD=Q1193564|1=Daiei (era)}} uprising (first half of the 16th century) and the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} military fire ({{ill|lt=Kishū Conquest#Hideyoshi's Kishu Attack|Q10855721|WD=Q10855721|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Kishu Attack|1=Battle of Kishuu}}). According to "Kanbunki" cited in "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}", in the early modern period it had two towns of Kanda (approximately 2 hectares). Although this Kanda existed until after the war, it is said to have disappeared due to {{ill|lt=agrarian reform|Q208128|WD=Q208128|1=Land reform}} in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}21 ({{ill|lt=1946|Q18610|WD=Q18610|1=1946}}). group="Note">However, in Kanbunki, it is written in the past tense, ``existed until recent times (omitted).'' . In addition, the same article mentions that the area around our company, approximately 500 {{ill|lt=stone (unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=stone|1=Koku}}, is called the ``Kami no Ki'' and is thought to be the remains of a vast shrine complex from ancient times, but the details are not clear. In "{{ill|lt=Ki Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964|WD=Q11604964|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki}}," the company at the time was the birthplace deity of the two villages of Udamori and Kitano, but it was originally considered to have been the birthplace deity of the entire Hirata Manor to which these two villages belonged, and it is assumed that due to the separation of the Konishi, Nishitai, and Kita villages due to a conflict within the manor, only the two villages became the birthplace.In April of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |Q134917284}}, and in 1880|Q7806}}, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (corner)|1985}}. Also, in 1911|Q2076}}, the Soja Shrine (formerly Village Shrine) located in Miyanoshi in the northern part of Wakayama City was {{ill|lt=combined enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}} (described later). After the war, it belonged to {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === God level === * Records of {{ill|lt=Divine rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in the history of the Six Kingdoms ** {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} On the 27th of the first month of the first year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), from the 8th grade of the junior fifth rank to the 8th grade of the lower rank of lower ranks (Junior 4th rank and lower rank of 8th rank) (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - The notation is ``Oya Tsuhime no Kami''. * After Six Kingdoms History ** Upper Juichii? (``{{ill|lt=Kinokuni Shinto Mecho|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008|1=Kii no Kuni Jinmyō-chō}}'') - Written as ``Oya Okami''. It is said to be an error in writing ``Junior Fourth Rank Upper.'' === About Soja Shrine, a joint shrine === Oyatsuhime Shrine is the Sosha Shrine (formerly Village Shrine) located in Miyanoshi, Kita Aza, Wakayama City, which was enshrined in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}44 ({{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076|1=1911}}). This Soja Shrine is a shrine that was also called ``Soja Daimyojin'' in early modern times, but the deity it enshrines is not clear (in ``Ki Meisho Zue'' it is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined) {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. {{ill|lt=Enkyo|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1746|Q7589|WD=Q7589|1=1746}}) "Nanki Shrine Record" and its subsequent "Ki Meisho Zue" indicate that the shrine name and enshrinement location is "Soja" in Kii. |Q1629088}} is close to the estimated location (present-day Fuchu, Wakayama City), so this Soja Shrine is believed to be {{ill|lt=Soja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} in Kii Province.{{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. On the other hand, ``Kii Zoku Fudoki'' denies this and states that the shrine that was enshrined in Nezuku Valley in Taisho Ochiai Village (present-day Ochiai, Wakayama City) was transferred from the Sōron of the Shōchu period {{Sfn|Soja Shrine Ruins (Taira)|1983|p=443}}. === Precincts === [[File:Oyatsuhime-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Shrine building]] As for the shrine building, the central main hall has an Oji-zukuri copper plate roof, {{ill|lt=facing worship|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970|1=kōhai}} is {{ill|lt=kara gable|Q977293|WD=Q977293|lt=kenkara gable|1=Karahafu}}, and {{jal {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1987|p=46-47}} where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chigi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Katsuoki|1=UNKNOWN}} are placed in the ridge of ink|Shinsha}}. To keep out the fog, all four sides of the shrine are covered with wooden walls. The side shrines on the left and right are also small-sized {{ill|lt=Nagazuzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with copper plate roofing and have chigi and bonito trees {{Sfn|Oyatsuhime Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1987|p=46-47}}. However, the Chigi of the left wakimiya (Isotake no Mikoto) is a soto-kege, and the chigi of the right wakimiya (Miyatomatohime no Mikoto) is an inward-kege. === Setsusha === [[File:Oyatsuhime-jinja keidaisha.JPG|thumb|200px|right|小箠]] '''Precincts end shrine''' * Small shrine '''Outside shrine''' * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitano, Wakayama City, {{Wiki coordinates|34|16|12.6|N|135|14|41.1|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|location|name=Outside shrine: Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine}}) === Ritual organization === === Shinto priest === The priesthood of Oyatsuhime Shrine was held for {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}}-{{ill|lt=1592|Q6769|WD=Q6769|1=1592}}) It is said that the Tsuchiya clan ({{ill|lt=real surname|Q11520116|WD=Q11520116|1=honsei}}: Fujiwara clan) has been hereditary since around 1987|1987|p=46-47}}. However, in the "Kiokuni Famous Places Zue," when it was compiled in the late Edo period, the Shinto priest was the Mori clan, who claimed to be a descendant of Oyahiko no Mikoto, and the current generation is the 83rd generation from Oyahiko no Mikoto. === Parishioners === Oyatsuhime Shrine is said to be the local deity of six districts, including Udamori, where it is enshrined, Hironishi, Kitano, Nango, Nishitai, and Kita. |Q11454763}} was formed, but currently it only remains in the Nagaho district. According to "Kii Zoku Fudoki", {{ill|lt=yabusame|Q1886157|WD=Q1886157|1=Yabusame}}, {{ill|lt=sarugaku|Q1204318|WD=Q1204318|1=Sarugaku}} and {{ill|lt=dengaku|Q3022455|WD=Q3022455|1=Dengaku}} were once held as festivals on June 1st and September 21st (both according to the lunar calendar). The same book also states that the priests of Itae-so Shrine serve during the festivals held on the last day of October and November 16th (both according to the lunar calendar), and that in October they serve food such as red bean rice, persimmon mochi, fish, and sake. It is said that a {{ill|lt=portable shrine|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} from Itaukeso Shrine was {{ill|lt=transferred|Q11561919|WD=Q11561919|1=transferred}} to our company in November. === Local '''Abbreviated genealogy of the three Ita-kei gods'''
The names of the gods are written in the Nihon Shoki, and the names of the shrines are written in the Engishiki. {{familytree/start|style=font-size:100%}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{ill|lt=Susano-o|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susa no Takashi|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
({{ill|lt=Susa Shrine (Arita City)|Q11664501|WD=Q11664501|lt=Susa Shrine|1=Susa Shrine (Q11664501)}})}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|+|-|-|.| }}{{familytree|border=0|01|02|03| 01={{ill|lt=50 Fierce God|Q10881556|WD=Q10881556|lt=50 Fierce Life|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}
